story.description: A clip of space, planets, solar activity, rockets, satellites, or astronomy: crescent moon phases captured through telescope
story.hook: The moon shows us one face for a reason.
story.lead: The moon shows us one face for a reason. Watch the moon's position, because its rotation and orbit are locked together. It spins once in about the same time it circles Earth, so the same side keeps facing us. That is synchronized motion, not stillness. Which orbit clue next?
story.script: The moon shows us one face for a reason. Watch the moon's position, because its rotation and orbit are locked together. It spins once in about the same time it circles Earth, so the same side keeps facing us. That is synchronized motion, not stillness. Which orbit clue next?

story.description: A clip of microscope footage, cells, microbes, algae, crystals, or tiny biology: glowing green microorganisms in motion
story.hook: Cells have to copy themselves before dividing.
story.lead: Cells have to copy themselves before dividing. Watch the cell boundary, because division only works after the internal instructions are copied and organized. Then the cell can split the material into two working lives. At this scale, reproduction is careful logistics. Which tiny process next?
story.script: Cells have to copy themselves before dividing. Watch the cell boundary, because division only works after the internal instructions are copied and organized. Then the cell can split the material into two working lives. At this scale, reproduction is careful logistics. Which tiny process next?

story.description: A clip of trees, roots, canopy, bark, or forests: sprawling oak tree in sunlit forest setting
story.hook: Deer can aim each ear toward danger
story.lead: Oaks warn each other through roots. Here's what blew my mind: their roots grow right into each other. That's why, when a deer starts chewing one, the connected trees get chemical signals. I just noticed their bark stays scuffed where the roots meet. You ever see moss on an oak trunk? That’s where the roots are closest. So next time you hug a tree, you might be standing in a warning line.
story.script: Oaks warn each other through roots. Here's what blew my mind: their roots grow right into each other. That's why, when a deer starts chewing one, the connected trees get chemical signals. I just noticed their bark stays scuffed where the roots meet. You ever see moss on an oak trunk? That’s where the roots are closest. So next time you hug a tree, you might be standing in a warning line.

story.description: A clip of weather, clouds, storms, lightning, snow, or sky phenomena: clouds moving fast in the sky time lapse in city
story.hook: Storm shows why the cloud spiral matters.
story.lead: Clouds aren't moving at all — it's the camera panning. That's why they streak like that. Those puffy ones travel 10 to 30 mph with the wind, but you'd never guess from their shape. Here's the part nobody tells you: if you could stand still in the sky, you'd see them crawl past like traffic. How fast do you think your local clouds usually drift?
story.script: Clouds aren't moving at all — it's the camera panning. That's why they streak like that. Those puffy ones travel 10 to 30 mph with the wind, but you'd never guess from their shape. Here's the part nobody tells you: if you could stand still in the sky, you'd see them crawl past like traffic. How fast do you think your local clouds usually drift?

story.description: A clip of nocturnal animals: vibrant nightlife at torre del caballito mexico city
story.hook: Magnets can show a hidden force map.
story.lead: Magnets can show a hidden force map. Watch the filings line up, because each tiny piece becomes a small magnet in the field. Together they trace the direction of the force around the magnet. The pattern is physics drawing itself. Which force next?
story.script: Magnets can show a hidden force map. Watch the filings line up, because each tiny piece becomes a small magnet in the field. Together they trace the direction of the force around the magnet. The pattern is physics drawing itself. Which force next?

story.description: A clip of space, planets, solar activity, rockets, satellites, or astronomy: earth
story.hook: The moon shows us one face for a reason.
story.lead: The moon shows us one face for a reason. Watch the moon's position, because its rotation and orbit are locked together. It spins once in about the same time it circles Earth, so the same side keeps facing us. That is synchronized motion, not stillness. Which orbit clue next?
story.script: The moon shows us one face for a reason. Watch the moon's position, because its rotation and orbit are locked together. It spins once in about the same time it circles Earth, so the same side keeps facing us. That is synchronized motion, not stillness. Which orbit clue next?
story.seo_title: The moon keeps one face turned toward Earth
story.title: The moon keeps one face turned toward Earth

trend_context.headline: Scientists Captured the Explosive Birth of a Solar Flare on Video - VICE
